text: The surgeon opens the lesser sac and gastrocolic ligament, carefully preserving the gastroepiploic arcade. Upon entering the lesser sac, a cystic lesion is visually identified.

The surgeon mobilizes the stomach from the antrum to the gastrosplenic ligament. A laparoscopic ultrasound is then used to assess the lesion for vascular involvement, clearly identifying the borders of the mass.

The surgeon identifies and isolates the splenic artery and vein from the posterior aspect of the pancreas using both superior and inferior approaches to the retropancreatic space. The infrapancreatic border is developed, and the retroperitoneal plane is opened. The site for proximal transection of the pancreas is identified at the level of the portal vein, achieved by alternating supra- and infrapancreatic approaches at the neck of the pancreas.

The surgeon dissects and skeletonizes the periportal region, carefully ligating all vascular connections between the pancreas and splenic vessels. A retropancreatic tunnel is created, and umbilical tape is passed through to aid in the manipulation of the distal pancreas.

The surgeon further isolates the pancreas from the splenic vasculature, then uses a stapling device to transect the pancreas. The distal staple line is excised and sent for frozen section analysis to confirm negative margins.
The surgeon closes the distal pancreatic duct with sutures, which are then used to facilitate further dissection of the surrounding tissue.

The surgeon carefully dissects along the tortuous path of the splenic vessels using a bipolar vessel sealer to minimize thermal spread and prevent vascular injury. As the dissection approaches the splenic hilum, the pancreas is observed dropping inferiorly, requiring delineation from the splenic vessels.
The surgeon oversews the proximal staple line of the pancreas, applying an adhesive polymer to seal the newly created distal aspect of the pancreas.
